name: Maven Central Repository API
what_it_does: The Maven Central Repository API provides search and metadata access for the world's largest Java/JVM artifact repository. Agents can search for artifacts by group, artifact ID, or class name; retrieve version listings; fetch POM metadata; and access artifact download URLs. The newer Central Portal API (central.sonatype.com) also supports programmatic publishing.
use_cases: Resolving the latest stable version of a Maven artifact for automated pom.xml or build.gradle updates
Searching Maven Central for libraries by keyword, class name, or group ID
Fetching POM metadata to analyze transitive dependencies for audit workflows
Verifying artifact existence and checksums before dependency pinning
Publishing artifacts to Maven Central via the Central Portal publishing API
not_for: Private artifact hosting (use JFrog Artifactory, Nexus Repository, or GitHub Packages)
Downloading large binary artifacts at high volume (use a local caching proxy like Nexus)
best_when: An agent needs to resolve, search, or audit Java/JVM dependencies for automated build tooling or security analysis.
avoid_when: You need private artifact storage, or are doing high-volume artifact downloads (use a repository manager as a proxy).

auth.notes: Read/search operations via search.maven.org are unauthenticated. The new Central Portal publishing API (central.sonatype.com/api) requires a user token (username+password pair generated in the portal). Legacy OSSRH publishing used Sonatype JIRA credentials. No OAuth flow. No scope granularity on tokens.

pricing.free_tier_limits: All search and read operations are free and unauthenticated. Publishing to Maven Central is free for open-source projects.
pricing.paid_tiers: None for open-source. Commercial Sonatype products (Nexus Lifecycle, etc.) are separate.
pricing.notes: Maven Central is a free community service. Publishing requires namespace verification (group ID ownership) but no payment. The Sonatype commercial product line is distinct from the free Maven Central service.

performance.rate_limits: No officially published rate limits for search API. Search.maven.org search API is Solr-backed and can be slow for complex queries. Artifact downloads are CDN-backed.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: The search API returns Solr-formatted JSON which is verbose but not designed for programmatic error handling. HTTP error codes are returned correctly but error bodies are minimal. The publishing API has better structured errors from the Central Portal.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Search and metadata GET operations are fully idempotent. Publishing an artifact version is idempotent if the same content is uploaded (content-addressed). Re-publishing a different artifact under the same GAV coordinates is rejected.
known_agent_gotchas: The search API uses Solr query syntax (q=g:org.springframework+a:spring-core) which is non-intuitive for agents generating queries
Group ID namespace ownership must be verified before first publish — this is a manual process that can take days
The old OSSRH publishing flow (oss.sonatype.org) is being sunset; new projects must use the Central Portal API
Maven artifact coordinates require three components (groupId:artifactId:version) — agents must parse and construct these correctly
SNAPSHOT versions are not hosted on Maven Central (only releases); SNAPSHOT lookups will 404
POM files must be downloaded separately from JARs; the API returns download URLs, not inline content
Checksum verification (SHA1, MD5) is expected by Maven tooling — agents publishing must compute and upload checksums

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Publishing tokens lack scope granularity. Namespace verification (group ID ownership) prevents the worst supply chain attacks. GPG signing of artifacts is required for publication, providing integrity guarantees for consumers. The transition from OSSRH to Central Portal improved security posture.

score_rationale: Maven Central's API is functional for Java ecosystem metadata retrieval but shows its age. The Solr-based search query syntax is not agent-friendly, documentation is scattered across old OSSRH and new Central Portal guides, and error messages are sparse. The namespace verification requirement for publishing is a multi-day manual process. Read-only artifact and version lookups work well; publishing workflows require significant setup and are best handled by human operators with agents assisting.
